data.title: Firefly Aerospace IPO: The Space Stock Everyone Will Be Talking About
data.description:
Introduction

Just last week, I covered Figma's (FIG) IPO and said it’s arguably the most-talked-about IPO of 2025. Today, I’ll say the hottest IPO of the year might not be Figma. It might just be Firefly Aerospace Inc. (NASDAQ:FLY). It’s hard to pick between the two, because they are so different, but I know for sure that FLY is on fire. Everyone’s talking about it, and there’s a very good reason for that - it’s playing in one of the most requested industries on the market, space.

Now, I am not talking about the vague, futuristic kind of space. Firefly already has contracts, launches, and money that very important clients are ready to pay. Even Firefly itself, in its SEC filing, points out that they operate in a "highly attractive and growing industry." And they’re right. According to a 2024 McKinsey report, the global space economy is expected to hit $1.8 trillion by 2035:

We operate in a highly attractive and growing industry. According to McKinsey’s report from 2024, the global space economy is projected to reach $1.8 trillion in value by 2035 driven by accelerating national security and commercial demand. In recent years, record demand for satellites caused a supply shortage of orbital launch vehicles. In 2024, nearly 2,800 satellites launched to orbit, representing more than a 500% increase in demand for launch services compared to just five years prior, according to a 2025 report by BryceTech. The scarcity of launch capacity and increasing government and commercial demand has led these customers to seek out defense technology companies with cost-efficient and proven production systems for increasing capacity.

And this doesn’t surprise me. We’re seeing more and more investors jump into anything that has to do with space, from satellites to Moon missions, to edge computing in orbit, whatever that can be useful in the future. The fascination with space isn’t new. We (humans) have always had this dream of reaching beyond our planet. The excitement is so huge because finally, the dreams, the tech, the money, and the ambition are lining up to make it possible.

Firefly is a company that literally just landed a spacecraft on the Moon and has active contracts with NASA, the U.S. Space Force, and a list of defense agencies. As a result, the stock is expensive, but if you're trying to apply regular stock valuation rules here, you're already looking at it the wrong way. The same as AI is now hot and expensive, space is as well.

If you try to find a cheap stock in this sector, chances are you’ll end up with a company that’s cheap for a reason, either no product, no revenues, or some kind of red flag. And if it’s expensive, well, that might just mean it’s working. Look at Rocket Lab (RKLB), or at Intuitive Machines (LUNR), these are direct public competitors of Firefly. Both of those are priced for perfection, both have their own issues, but people still buy them.

So, it's not surprising that Firefly was priced at $45 per share for its IPO, but the stock soared to $70 on the opening. That’s a massive 55%. That kind of move confirms what I said earlier: this IPO is one of the most hyped of the year, and investors are ready to jump in for a pure-play space exposure, even if it's expensive.

For what it’s worth, this isn’t the first time I’ve written about the space race. If you’re curious about the bigger picture, check out my article on Amazon’s (AMZN) Project Kuiper. In that research, you will find many similar points, especially about risks and regulations, and you’ll see that it’s all connected.

About the IPO itself, most of them get a dramatic start in the beginning. We usually get a big spike on day one, and then the stock pulls back. That could very well happen here, too. But that doesn’t change the bigger story. Firefly is a very interesting stock and definitely the one to keep on your watchlist.

Near-Death to Lunar Landing

Firefly has an interesting and very special history. It was founded in 2014, but then filed for bankruptcy just three years later, and was “rescued” by Ukrainian entrepreneur Max Polyakov. But even that got complicated because the U.S. government forced Polyakov to divest due to national security concerns. So the company kept changing hands, rebuilding itself, and somehow lived to see this IPO.

Then, in March 2025, Firefly’s Blue Ghost lander touched down on the Moon, which was a massive achievement. This is what the company wrote the moment it happened:

March 2, 2025, 2:34 am CST: Successful Touchdown Confirmed

We have confirmation Blue Ghost stuck the landing! Firefly just became the first commercial company in history to achieve a fully successful Moon landing. This small step on the Moon represents a giant leap in commercial exploration.

In that moment, Firefly stopped being just another “dreaming” startup and became a real competitor of SpaceX, Rocket Lab, and Intuitive Machines. Firefly officially joined the space race.

More Than Just Moon Landing

Firefly is building a full stack of space infrastructure. They have the Alpha rocket, which is:

Firefly’s small-lift rocket, Alpha, is equipped to launch more than 1,000 kg to low Earth orbit in support of commercial, civil, and national security missions. The flight-proven vehicle is designed to be a responsive, reliable, and cost-competitive launch option, enabling direct, on-demand deliveries when and where customers need to fly.

It’s already operational for some time now. More than a year ago, there was news that Firefly Aerospace successfully launched Alpha FLTA005 Noise for NASA.

They’re working on a bigger vehicle, Elytra, which is being developed alongside Lockheed Martin and is designed to be reusable. They are getting ready for their first mission, which is a test flight. It’s scheduled for some time this year.

Firefly’s multi-mission Elytra vehicles offer in-space maneuverability and servicing when and where customers need it. Our line of highly mobile and scalable spacecraft provides on-demand payload delivery, imaging, long-haul communications, and domain awareness across cislunar space. Each configuration – Elytra Dawn, Elytra Dusk, and Elytra Dark – travels further into space to support larger payloads and more advanced services for both government and commercial customers.

Then they’ve got a lunar lander, which I already mentioned, it’s called Blue Ghost. Besides, they’re developing Ocula, which is a Lunar Imaging Service, which will help identify possible landing zones, mineral deposits on the surface of the Moon, etc.

So, besides launching rockets, they’re building vehicles, landers, satellites, and even a platform for secure data transfer in space.

Who’s Buying?

Firefly isn’t building a space tourism company, although they do have commercial ambitions (especially in satellite tech and lunar missions), but their current customer base consists of government agencies and other important organizations. For example, the U.S. government, NASA, Space Force, the Department of Defense, the NRO, and giants like Lockheed Martin and L3Harris. These are serious customers, with serious budgets.

In Q1 2025, Firefly brought in $55.9 million in revenue. That’s more than six times what they made a year ago. Their backlog is more than $1.1 billion, spread across over 30 missions. That includes launches, lunar landings, and upcoming satellite work:

Strong customer demand backs our financial profile with approximately $1.1 billion in backlog as of March 31, 2025 and multi-launch agreements across our product lines. Underpinning our financial profile is the combination of efficient contract structure and milestone-based billing. Before launch, we typically have collected approximately 90% of the total contract value, which is highly advantageous as production ramps.

It Is Expensive. But So Is Everything Else in Space

Now, the valuation is high. At $6.3 billion post-IPO, Firefly is trading at a very rich price-to-sales multiple if you look at annualized Q1 numbers. They’re losing money, $60 million in Q1 2025 alone, and their margins are still negative.

Before the IPO, they had over $170 million in cash and about $150 million in total debt.

To calculate the approximate Price to Sales ratio, I take IPO numbers first, and then I'll look at the post-IPO as well: Market Cap of $6.3 billion, and Q1 2025 revenue of $55.9 million. Let’s assume this is their new level now (they have $1.1 billion in a backlog, after all), and take the Annualized Revenue Estimate of $55.9 million × 4 = $223.6 million. P/S Ratio = Market Cap / Annualized Revenue​ = 223.6 million / 6.3 billion​ ≈ 28.2x. I assume the stock has the chance to come back to the pre-IPO valuation when the excitement calms down a little.

But until that happens, and the stock is at about $70 per share, the valuation looks much more expensive. That $6.3 billion market cap I used earlier, now got closer to $10 billion. That puts Firefly at a price-to-sales ratio of around 44x based on annualized Q1 numbers.

28 times is already a lot, not to mention 44 times. But again, that’s not crazy in this sector. Rocket Lab is trading at the same sky-high multiples - P/S of about 47x TTM. Intuitive Machines is much cheaper now, with a price-to-sales ratio of less than 4x, but LUNR has had some problems lately. When the stock was at its high, its P/S multiple was much higher than it is right now. Something like ~10x, if I am not mistaken.

This entire industry is valued based on potential, not profit. So here, you have to think about what it could become, and how far ahead of competitors it already is. I don’t think we will have the chance to buy it really cheap. LUNR level is a bargain compared to peers, but again, they had some problems, so am I personally jumping on this opportunity - not really. Although I did some nice trades with LUNR in the past. If the execution is perfect, you will be paying too much. Discount comes with issues.

Where the Money’s Going

Out of the $868 million they just raised, some will go toward cleaning up the balance sheet. They’ve got debt to pay down and preferred shares to redeem, like most young aerospace companies do. But the bigger picture here is that the majority of this money will be sponsoring future growth.

They’re already investing in expanding their rocket and lander programs, especially Blue Ghost, which already made it to the Moon and now has a second mission scheduled. They’re improving their launch infrastructure and scaling their manufacturing capabilities, because the demand is clearly coming. Besides, they've got the Ocula satellite system, which needs funding as well.

Risks

Here we have the same risk that we have for all of the other space companies: execution. We’ve seen it before, and one failed launch, one delay, one issue, and the stock can crash. This is what actually went wrong for Intuitive Machines when their lander landed on its side. The second time it happened, or in March, the stock crashed in a matter of a couple of days from $20 per share to $6. This can happen to any of these companies, so beware. On the other hand, when they have a successful launch, the stock will soar as well. So, I guess, this industry is not for the faint-hearted.

Conclusion

A 55% jump on day one is not small, and a lot of future growth is already priced in now. It means any misstep will hurt the stock, but any strong execution will still be rewarded. That’s the double-edged sword of hot IPOs in hot sectors.

So, Firefly isn’t a cheap stock, it’s not a profitable stock, and it’s definitely not a safe stock. But it’s one of the most exciting ones we have seen lately. Will it be volatile? Absolutely, the confirmation of it you can already see today, on their first trading day. I think there is a strong chance we will see a post-IPO drop. Now, what to do with this information is up to you. Someone will decide to open a position now, and DCA it down. Someone will wait and hope to catch it lower. Someone won't buy it and will call it a meme stock.

And all of them will be a little right. This is the kind of stock that will move 20% in a day on news. But if you’re looking for exposure to the space industry, and you’re willing to take on the risk that comes with that, Firefly is one of the clearest pure-play bets you can make right now. If they execute well, today’s high multiple might look a lot more reasonable a few years from now.

data.title: Natural Gas Holds Near $3.10 As Triangle Consolidation And EU Oversupply Limit Upside (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Natural gas prices are consolidating near $3.10, following a technical rebound from the $2.85–$2.95 support zone that marked a key inflection area over the past several months. The bounce, which came after a direct test of the rising trendline from September 2024, has helped stabilize sentiment in the near term.

However, broader momentum remains constrained, with the commodity continuing to compress within a symmetrical triangle formation as traders await a breakout. Overhead resistance remains dense, with the 20-, 50-, and 100-day EMAs capping rallies between $3.17 and $3.43.

Weak momentum and oversupply limit bullish conviction

Technically, the daily RSI for NG has recovered to 44.14 but remains below neutral levels, reinforcing the lack of immediate upside thrust. A move above 50 on the RSI could strengthen the case for a test of the triangle’s descending resistance near $3.45, though failure to clear this level would risk another retest of the $2.90 handle. Should price fall below $2.80, the bias would likely shift toward the $2.30 support zone, which previously contained multiple retracements in early 2024.

Fundamentally, oversupply concerns in Europe remain a key drag. Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and the prospect of U.S. sanctions targeting Russian energy flows, European gas futures have dipped below €34/MWh. Storage buildup remains healthy, with Italy and France surpassing 79% capacity. Norwegian pipeline flows and LNG imports have added to the surplus, while reduced demand from Asia has kept interregional LNG competition muted. This dynamic continues to weigh on natural gas futures, curbing the scope for a near-term breakout despite technical support holding.

Outlook hinges on breakout resolution and storage trends

With price wedged between converging trendlines and seasonal supply factors still at play, natural gas appears poised for a larger move heading into September. A confirmed breakout above $3.45 could spark bullish momentum toward $3.80 or higher, while a breakdown below $2.85 would likely trigger a shift in sentiment and a deeper correction. For now, traders remain focused on both storage trends and geopolitical signals that may tilt the balance.

In our prior update, we noted natural gas price action was coiling within a multi-month triangle. The recent bounce confirmed the lower support band, but the lack of volume and upside momentum continues to delay a decisive move. Until that changes, traders should expect rangebound price behavior.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Central Garden & Pet Company: No Sales Growth In Sight
data.description:
Central Garden & Pet Company (NASDAQ:CENTA) manufactures and distributes products related to garden and pet supplies as the company’s name strongly suggests. CENTA owns a portfolio of good consumer staples brands in the categories, including names such as Amdro, Cadet, Four Paws, Kaytee, Nylabone, and several others.

While CENTA has had attractive long-term returns, the stock has now stagnated for several years. The company’s revenue growth has turned negative from a previously good level due to a lack of M&A and a weak organic performance, still continuing with very weakness in the fiscal Q3 report. Earnings have benefited massively from the Cost and Simplicity program, but efficiency improvements may not be enough to sustain earnings momentum for long.

CENTA’s Weak Growth Continues in Q3

CENTA’s revenue growth has stalled and even turned into an accelerating decline. After a history of constant acquisitions that bolstered the company’s wide brand portfolio, revenues first turned towards a slight decline in FY2023 and have remained on a similar path since.

The fiscal Q3 report followed the trend. CENTA’s revenues came in at $961 million, down by -4% year-on-year with a nearly similar decline in both the Pet and Garden segments. The result disappointed Wall Street’s consensus by $35 million, as CENTA doesn’t seem to be even near a sales growth turnaround.

The Pet segment’s weakness reflects CENTA’s product assortment rationalization as the company has cut lower-margin durable products and customers. On top, weak consumer confidence seems to have weighed slightly on sales as CENTA relates to soft demand, hitting durables especially hard as CENTA’s Pet portfolio is concentrating increasingly on consumables. At the same time, the Garden segment was pressured by cool and rainy weather during Q3, on top of which CENTA lost two product lines in the segment’s distribution business. Altogether, there seemed to be many factors still impacting sales strength in Q3.

Positively, CENTA still notes having maintained market share in the Pet segment, and even noted market share gains in several product categories in the Garden segment; recent revenue weakness mostly reflects an industry-wide downturn. As comparisons, The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM) reported a -1% organic revenue decline in the latest report, and Spectrum Brands Holdings, Inc. (SPB) reported a very steep -11.1% organic sales decline amid slowing shipments and slow demand – the operating environment clearly hasn’t been easy. Both of the mentioned major competitors have also reported very slow growth in the past few years.

Ultimately, it still doesn’t seem that CENTA’s sales performance is improving anytime soon. The company’s long-term target is only set at low single-digit revenue growth, but even achieving such growth appears to be challenging as sales continue to decline. CENTA still relates to a poor Q4 outlook amid consumer confidence pressure.

Rising private label penetration poses a risk to CENTA’s branded product portfolio, and while CENTA also manufactures private label products for retailers, the company won’t capture as great margins from the category. CENTA notes increasing humanization of pets as a long-term industry tailwind, but at the same time, the share of pet-owning households has declined from 54% in 2019 to 51% in 2023.

Cost and Simplicity Program Continues to Improve Earnings For Now

CENTA still has a lot of positives to note in the earnings performance – the Q3 adjusted EPS reached $1.56, growing by $0.24 year-on-year and beating Wall Street’s consensus by $0.13. Adjusted operating income came in at $135 million, up by 9% through a 170 basis point margin improvement.

Behind the earnings increase, CENTA’s multi-year Cost and Simplicity program has driven significant gross margin gains and offset negative operating leverage in operating expenses. CENTA’s product assortment rationalization has improved the product margin mix, and manufacturing consolidation has improved efficiency on top of other initiatives. Representatively, the gross margin showed a 280 basis point improvement to 34.6% in the latest report.

The productivity program has already driven CENTA’s operating margin to a new long-term record level – CENTA’s weak sales are partially a fair compromise as the company has clearly optimized the product portfolio performance to a much better performance. A history of M&A previously made the organization too complex, and the program is still ongoing and seems to be driving further margin gains in the future as well. Yet, over the long term, I believe that CENTA’s sales performance will be more critical for the earnings outlook as declining sales need to be met with constant cost cuts to salvage margins – SG&A increased by 30 basis points as a percentage of sales in Q3, driving negative operating leverage as sales shrink.

Valuation

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model to estimate a fair value for CENTA. After macroeconomic weakness subsides and CENTA ends its product assortment rationalization, I estimate sales growth to rebound to a slow 1% perpetual level. The estimate suggests growth slightly below average inflation.

While CENTA’s margins are improving, I ultimately assume the margin will moderate due to weak sales growth. I expect the EBIT margin to stabilize at a fairly good 8% level. CENTA’s after-tax cash flow conversion should be very good as the company doesn’t have a significant need for capital expenditures.

The estimates put CENTA’s fair value at $38.11, 15% above the CENTA listing’s price at the time of writing. The valuation currently provides a moderate margin of safety, but for good reason as there currently isn’t clarity for a sales growth turnaround.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.49%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.82% interest rate, annualized from CENTA’s Q2 interest expenses and debt. I assume a fairly high 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.25% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the U.S. I use Aswath Damodaran’s 0.90 beta estimate for the household products industry. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity stands at 8.66% and the WACC at 7.49%.

Takeaway

CENTA’s Q3 report still showed very weak sales without visibility for a growth turnaround; macroeconomic pressure and low general category growth are still weighing on the company. Yet, the Cost and Simplicity program is driving significant margin improvements, pushing up earnings despite weak sales. The longer-term outlook is still more dependent on a return to sales growth, though – efficiency can’t be improved forever. The stock is valued with a fair margin of safety, and as such, I initiate CENTA at a Hold rating.

data.title: Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

U.S. small-cap stocks ended the quarter with sizable gains, but the march forward was volatile. Initially, stocks turned lower after President Trump unveiled tariff rates for countries that were much higher than investors had anticipated. However, equities then rebounded sharply, as the President’s economic team appeared to be softening its stance on tariffs. For the quarter, the Russell 2500™ Index was up 8.59%, while the Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund—Investor Class gained 0.45%.

Much of the Fund’s underperformance relative to the benchmark was due to our long holdings, which collectively did not keep pace with the Index. As investor views on tariffs shifted, a “risk-on” environment followed for much of the quarter. Against this backdrop, the stocks of lower-quality companies and those with more speculative business models generally outperformed those of higher-quality businesses. This was a headwind for the Fund’s long portfolio, given that our investment style focuses on finding high-quality companies. And that headwind was severe. By some metrics this was one of the strongest environments favoring low-quality stocks that we’ve experienced.

For perspective, one way we gauge whether high-quality or low-quality stocks are in favor is through a proprietary process, in which we screen and rank the small-cap universe by several aggregate factors, including financial metrics such as return on assets and operating profit margins, among other items. We then compare how the companies in the top quintile of this aggregate score (high-quality businesses) perform against the bottom quintile (low-quality businesses). Based on that metric, this was the third-strongest 10-week period of low-quality outperformance of the last decade, and the strongest since early 2021. Given that headwind, we weren’t surprised to see our long portfolio underperform.

Details of the quarter

Among our long positions, the largest detractor from Fund performance was Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY). The company is one of the largest homebuilders in North America, specializing in modular homes for the single-family, multi-family and hospitality segments. Champion’s stock was down after company management reported weaker than expected quarterly operating results. High interest rates make home payments more expensive, so some of Champion’s customers are offsetting that expense by opting out of some of the higher-grade amenities within modular houses. This is a headwind for Champion’s earnings because those amenities tend to improve margins for the company. The short-term results haven’t changed our view of the company. In an environment where homebuilders face significant challenges, we were pleased to see Champion increase revenue 10.7% in its most recent quarter over the same period a year ago.

Another detractor was National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA), a real-estate investment trust that owns and operates self-storage facilities. The outlook of higher-for-longer interest rates likely weighed on the stock. For National Storage, higher rates reduce home sales, which in turn reduces the need for storage space. However, we remain confident in the company’s long-term outlook and maintained our position. We like National Storage for several reasons: Its market is large and growing. The company also has opportunities to acquire smaller competitors. Additionally, we’re impressed with National Storage’s management team, which we’ve known for many years.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc. (INSP) also detracted. The company develops minimally invasive solutions for patients with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). The second quarter saw the long-awaited launch of Inspire V, the company’s new device that greatly simplifies the medical installation procedure. The stock likely sold off because medical professionals will need to work through their inventory of Inspire IV prior to adopting the new product. Inspire V extends the company’s lead in the OSA space and should result in higher volume across its install base in the second half the year.

One of the largest contributors on the long side was Fabrinet (FN). Fabrinet makes optical components that are used in data centers. The business benefits from the increasing use of artificial intelligence (AI). While fundamentals for the company remain strong, AI has been a popular investment theme and that likely played a larger role in fueling the stock’s rise.

Another contributor was Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE), an enterprise software provider for the property and casualty insurance industry. Guidewire has continued to benefit as insurance companies have increasingly replaced their core (usually on-premises) legacy software with centralized, cloud-based offerings. We’ve been impressed with how Guidewire’s technology suite is gaining traction among insurers.

HealthEquity, Inc. (HQY) also contributed. The company is the largest U.S. non-bank custodian for health savings accounts (HSAs). HealthEquity’s stock rebounded after having lost ground in late March and April. At that time, management said it faced higher service costs as it sought to prevent fraud and cybersecurity threats. Like other financial firms, HealthEquity has experienced an increase in such threats. But after speaking with management, we felt the steps they’ve been taking to address fraud and cyber threats are sensible. We’ve also been pleased to see the company prioritize various security initiatives that were already under way to help mitigate the problem. Additionally, we’ve been encouraged by the strength of HealthEquity’s underlying business trends.

Turning to short positions, detractors from Fund performance are those stocks that rose in price. One of the largest detractors was Rocket Lab Corp. (RKLB), a space company that designs, manufactures and launches rockets and spacecraft. Investors have been enthusiastic about companies tied to space exploration, in part due to SpaceX’s innovation around reusable rockets. However, we don’t see any competitive advantages for Rocket Lab and believe the company’s path to growth is far from certain.

Among shorts, contributors to Fund performance are those stocks that declined in price. One of the largest contributors was Sweetgreen, Inc. (SG), a fast-casual restaurant chain that specializes in made-to-order salads and grain bowls. We short the position as a hedge against some of our long positions in the consumer discretionary and consumer staples sectors. Such companies can be impacted when consumer spending turns weak. This quarter, the stock was likely down due to fears that a potential trade war could lead to inflation and a tougher spending environment. (Current and future holdings are subject to change and risk.)

Outlook and positioning

While the Fund underperformed its benchmark, we remain constructive about the positioning of our long holdings. As we mentioned earlier, in the second quarter’s strong risk-on environment, lower quality stocks outperformed. Typically, high-quality stocks trade at valuations higher than low-quality stocks, but the gap in valuations between the two has narrowed. We believe that will translate into higher expected returns for our brand of high-quality investing over the next three-to-five years.

We acknowledge that markets can remain speculative and would hesitate to predict exactly when the trend favoring lower-quality stocks will reverse. But we would also point out that as we head toward the back half of 2025, there is still a great deal of uncertainty about tariffs and trade policy, a war in Europe and tensions in the Middle East. Throw in the uncertainty about interest rate policy and there are plenty of factors that could shake the equity markets and sour investor sentiment. We’ve tilted the Fund’s portfolio a little more defensively in light of these risks, targeting a market beta of 0.50 for the portfolio, down from 0.55 last quarter.

Amid the uncertainties, we’re hewing closely to the investment philosophy and process that has always guided the Wasatch investment team. Within our long portfolio, we remain focused on high-quality companies that have a proven track record of earnings growth, a history of strong free cash flow generation, and that are led by management teams we know and trust. That high-quality focus was admittedly a headwind to the Fund’s performance relative to the Russell 2500 Index in the second quarter. But in times of macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, these are precisely the types of businesses we want to own.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Mick Rasmussen

Equity investing involves risks, including potential loss of the principal amount invested. Short selling incurs significant unique risks, including potentially unlimited downside risk, high short-sale related expenses, and unavailability of securities to sell short, among others, all of which could negatively impact the performance of the Fund. Additionally, the Fund may not be able to borrow the securities it intends to sell short.

The Fund’s investments in long and short equity positions expose it to changes in the value of securities, which exceed the value of the Fund’s assets. Investment in the Fund will involve market risks associated with different types of investment decisions than those made for a typical “long only” fund. The Fund relies on quantitative models which entail unique risks, including the risk that a model may be limited or incorrect. These risks are described in more detail in the prospectus.

An investor should consider investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, containing this and other information, visit wasatchglobal.com or call 800.551.1700. Please read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Information in this document regarding market or economic trends, or the factors influencing historical or future performance, reflects the opinions of management as of the date of this document. These statements should not be relied upon for any other purpose.

The Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund’s investment objective is long-term growth of capital. CFA® is a trademark owned by the CFA Institute.

† The Russell 2500 Index is a market-cap weighted index that includes the smallest 2,500 small- and mid-cap stocks covered in the broad-based Russell 3000 of U.S.-based listed equities.

†† The FTSE 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index is intended to track the daily performance of 3-month U.S. Treasury bills. Indexes are unmanaged.

Investors cannot invest in these or any indexes.

The Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund has been developed solely by Wasatch Global Investors. The Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund is not in any way connected to or sponsored, endorsed, sold or promoted by the London Stock Exchange Group plc and its group undertakings (collectively, the “LSE Group”). FTSE Russell is a trading name of certain of the LSE Group companies.

All rights in the Russell 2500 Index and the FTSE 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index vest in the relevant LSE Group company, which owns these indexes. Russell ® is a trademark of the relevant LSE Group company and is used by any other LSE Group company under license.

These indexes are calculated by or on behalf of FTSE International Limited or its affiliate, agent or partner. The LSE Group does not accept any liability whatsoever to any person arising out of (a) the use of, reliance on or any error in these indexes or (b) investment in or operation of the Wasatch Long/Short Alpha Fund or the suitability of these indexes for the purpose to which they are being put by Wasatch Global Investors.

Beta is a quantitative measure of the volatility of a given security relative to the overall market. A beta above one is more volatile than the overall market, while a beta below one is less volatile.

The “cloud” is the internet. Cloud-computing is a model for delivering information-technology services in which resources are retrieved from the internet through web-based tools and applications, rather than from a direct connection to a server.

Earnings growth is a measure of growth in a company’s net income over a specific period, often one year.

Operating margin, also called operating profit margin, is the amount a company makes on every dollar of sales after deducting variable costs but before accounting for interest or taxes. Operating margin is calculated by dividing the company’s operating income by its net sales.

Return on assets (ROA) measures a company’s profitability by showing how many dollars of earnings a company derives from each dollar of assets it controls.

“Risk-on” is when investors are seeking the potentially higher returns of riskier assets and put money back in the market willing to risk the money, thus risk on.

Valuation is the process of determining the current worth of an asset or company.

data.title: Eli Lilly Q2 Earnings: Ignore Oral Weight Loss Bombshell, This Is A Buy The Dip Moment
data.description:
Investment Overview

Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), the world's largest pharmaceutical globally by market cap, announced its Q2 earning earlier today, exceeding the Street's expectations on revenues of $15.6bn, and on adjusted earnings per share of $6.31, and hiking full-year revenue guidance from $58bn - $61bn, to $60bn - $62bn.

Unfortunately for the Indianapolis-headquartered Pharma, earnings outperformance was overshadowed by the release of worse than expected data from >3k patient study of its oral weight loss pill, Orforglipron, which could "only" achieve an average of 12.4% mean weight loss (27.3 lbs), compared to 0.9% in the placebo arm. Analysts had apparently hoped to see reductions of >15%.

It's pretty clear which bit of news the market has homed in on, as Lilly stock has crashed by >15% in trading so far today, to ~$630 per share, which gives the company a market cap valuation of just over $600bn, it's lowest value in over 18 months.

Meanwhile, shares of Novo Nordisk (NVO), Lilly's great rival in the glucagon-like peptide-1 ("GLP-1") space, have gained >5% in trading so far today - Novo hopes to secure approval for an oral version of its Wegovy (semaglutide) weight loss drug before the end of this year having already had its formal submission accepted by the FDA.

Before we consider the orforglipron data in more detail, let's firstly review Lilly's - overall impressive - Q2 results.

Eli Lilly Q2 Results Overview - 35% Sequential Growth For Tirzepatide A "Wow" Moment

Lilly's Q2 revenue figure of $15.6bn was up an impressive 38% year-on-year, somewhat justifying the massive hype around its tirzepatide drug, which is in fact a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) mimetic - Novo's semaglutide targets GLP-1 only.

Lilly says that its "incretin analogs" (incretin is a hormone involved stimulating insulin release and reducing glucagon secretion) share of the market has increased to 57% of total prescriptions i.e. tirzepatide - which is approved to treat type 2 diabetes under the brand name Mounjaro, and to treat obesity under the brand name Zepbound - is outselling semaglutide - approved in the same two indications as Ozempic / Wegovy.

In Q2, Mounjaro and Zepbound earned respectively $5.2bn and $3.4bn revenues, up 68% and 172$ year-on-year respectively, and up 35% and 46% sequentially. The two meds together accounted for ~55% of Eli Lilly's revenues, underlining the importance of tirzepatide to the company, its future, and its share price and valuation.

Altogether, Lilly's cardiometabolic division earned $11.34bn of revenues, up 51% year-on-year, with T2D med Trulicity earning $1.1bn of revenues, down 12% year-on-year, and Jardiance (heart failure) and Humalog (human insulin) earning $503m and $690m respectively, down 20% and 10% respectively.

Breast cancer med Verzenio earned $1.5bn revenues, up 12% year-on-year, helping Lilly's oncology division earn $2.4bn revenues, or 16% of total revenues, while Taltz sales of $848m, and Olumiant sales of $246m, up 3% and 8%, helped the immunology division generate $1.3bn revenues. The neuroscience division earned just $344m of revenues, with Alzheimer's Disease drug Kisunla, once believed to have double-digit billion peak revenue potential, earned $49m, up from $22m in Q1.

Lilly racked up total operating expenses of $6.24bn, earning operating income of $6.9m (margin of 44%), while net income came to $5.7bn (margin of 37%), and earnings per shares ("EPS") came to $6.29. Full 2024 guidance was shared as per the below slide, taken from today's earnings presentation:

As we can see, EPS has been hiked, as well as revenues, meaning the current forward price to sales and price to earnings ratios for Lilly, based on current share price and valuation, are ~9.9x, and 29x, which is high for the "Big Pharma" sector. Sector averages by my estimation are typically <5x, and <20x.

Eli Lilly's current dividend pays $1.5 per quarter, which translates to an annual yield of ~0.8%.

Orforglipron Bombshell Adds To Growing Fears Around Lilly's Obesity Prospects

Until today, it had been widely accepted that Lilly's portfolio of weight loss oriented GLP drugs was streets ahead of the competition - even Novo Nordisk.

Novo's semaglutide lost out to tirzepatide in a head-to-head study (albeit one conducted by Lilly), and its next generation drug Cagrisema - a combination of cagrilintide, a dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist, and semaglutide - flopped in a Phase 3 study, achieving mean 15.7% weight loss at 68 weeks, when Novo had talked up its chances of achieving >25%.

Orforglipron data shared in April from a Phase 3 study showed that it reduced A1C by an average of 1.3% to 1.6% across doses, and a secondary endpoint showed that it "reduced weight by an average 16.0 lbs (7.9%) at the highest dose in a key secondary endpoint" at 40 weeks.

The data set up an approval shot, and the chances are, orforglipron will still be approved, most likely next year, but the latest data suggests it may not be nearly as appealing to patients as was once expected.

While oral administration may be preferable to many, those using injectable Zepbound / Mounjaro, can theoretically lose >20% of their body fat, compared to 12.4% using orforglipron over the same timeframe, while oral Wegovy may be the outstanding option as patients in clinical studies achieved more or less the same results as those using injectable Wegovy - >15%.

To make matters even worse for Lilly, potentially, a recent study of higher dose semaglutide (7.2mg) showed that patients lost a mean 20.7% weight at 68 weeks - matching torzeaptide's pivotal study results.

Another threat to Lilly's weight loss business comes from a different source - well, two different sources in fact. Firstly, CVS Health (CVS) the health insurance and pharmacy benefit manager ("PBM"), has dropped tirzepatide from its preferred formulary lists, and opted to prioritize coverage of semaglutide instead.

Secondly, companies such as Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), the online pharmacy, continue to sell "compounded" versions of both semaglutide and tirzepatide, i.e. non FDA approved versions of the drug, exploiting a loophole in policy that allows it to provide a compounded version if a patient has a "personalized" dose requirement.

On its earnings call today, Lilly's CEO David Ricks told analysts that law enforcement and the FDA need to "step up their game" in order to put an end to this process, owing to the risk posed to patients by non-FDA-approved versions of GLP drugs; however, an alternative solution might be for Lilly to drop its price for Zepbound / Mounjaro, potentially making compounders unprofitable, given they cannot compete in terms of economies of scale.

Lilly is already selling tirzepatide at low prices via its DTC channels, and that is a strategy that may become increasingly important in the face of President Trump's war on drug pricing, as it cuts out the much disliked PBM - another one of Trump's targets.

Drug Pricing Pressures / Tariffs Also Give Eli Lilly Headache

Lilly's management could be forgiven for reaching out for some of its migraine therapy, Emgality - $201m sales in Q2 - when contemplating the Trump administration's threat to both introduce a "most favored nation" ("MFN") drug pricing policy - i.e. Americans pay the same amount for their drugs as whichever nation globally charges the least - and slap tariffs of up to 250% on pharmaceutical imports.

Lilly has announced plans to take its investment in domestic manufacturing to the $50bn mark since 2020, with four new manufacturing sites planned, creating >3k new jobs in the process, but the latest demand from the government to implement MFN within 60 days - a demand that almost certainly cannot be met without Pharma valuations coming crashing down, if at all - puts enormous pressure on the sector, and may spell the beginning of the end of double-digit profit margins, let alone the 37% margin reported by Lilly today. With that said, Trump tried and failed to implement an MFN policy in his first term.

Looking Ahead - Should Investors Be Looking To Buy, Sell, or Hold LLY Stock After Today's News?

If we take a look at Lilly's current clinical pipeline of new drug prospects, we can see that it is somewhat low on new molecular entities, and arguably, somewhat high on weight loss / diabetes:

Imlunestrant - a selective estrogen receptor degrader ("SERD") indicated for breast cancer, is an intriguing prospect with "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) potential, and remternetug a potential improvement on Kisunla, which has a difficult safety profile to sell to patients. Jayprica, in heme cancers, has multi-billion-dollar potential, but otherwise, it is mostly within the twin indications of weight loss / T2D where Lilly will be judged.

In fairness, bulls may look at the >$8.5bn tirzepatide earned in a single quarter, and the >$60bn Lilly hopes to generate in revenues in 2025, which would place it behind only Pfizer (PFE) among global Pharmaceutical companies, and wonder what all the fuss is about? Analysts at Goldman's recently downgraded their expectations for the global obesity market, but still believe that it will be worth >$95bn by 2030, and Lilly currently controls >50% of that market.

Tirzepatide will very likely soon be the world's best-selling drug - I'd estimate it may generate >$33bn revenues this year - and has a strong chance of securing further approvals in e.g. cardiovascular and kidney disease, and longer-term perhaps even in Alzheimer's and even oncology, analysts have speculated - it is a unique and extremely valuable drug.

It's also worth mentioning Lilly's recent acquisition of Verve Therapeutics, working on an in-vivo cholesterol lowering therapy that, if successful, would almost certainly become a double-digit billion selling asset. And finally, worth mentioning is retatrutide, Lilly's pipeline GLP-1 that targets GIP, GLP, and glucagon, earning it the nickname "Triple G" (tirzepatide is sometimes referred to as "twincretin" due to its dual mechanism of action). Early retatrutide data has been compelling, and more is due before the end of year, the company has promised.

In my last note on Lilly, published in June, I suggested that "if you don't buy on weight loss promise now, you probably never will" and wrote:

In order for Lilly to fulfill my prior prediction and achieve a market cap of >$1 trillion, you truly have to believe that GLP-1 drugs are like nothing we have ever seen before, and capable of drumming up >$150bn per annum in revenues for Lilly, I'd speculate—a forward price to sales ratio of ~6.7x (based on a $1 trillion valuation).

You also must believe that Lilly can manage the price of its drug while still making a high profit margin, >40%, I'd speculate, and maintaining a price-to-earnings ratio of <15x, even as its valuation creeps towards $1 trillion.

Tirzepatide earned $6.2bn of revenues in Q1 2025, and that figure would need to surpass $20bn per quarter, combined with Lilly's other GLP-1 drugs, in order to support a long-term bull thesis for Lilly stock, in my view.

Some of these figures may seem ridiculous today, especially after a comparatively poor showing for orforglipron, but then again, it's said that one in three Americans - >100m people - are obese and would benefit from treatment with a GLP-1 drug. As I wrote in a recent note on Novo Nordisk:

therefore, if Novo charges $1,000 per annum - one tenth of what it charges today—and enjoys a 50% market share, it will earn $50bn per annum - in the U.S. alone.

The same can be said of Eli Lilly - once you begin doing the math, a revenue target of ~$150bn per annum begins to seem more and more reasonable.

Investing in Lilly today is a highly subjective matter, and my trillion dollar market cap target seems an awfully long way off, however $20bn of revenue per quarter could be achieved almost by this time next year, based on current growth rates, and if this is not a bull thesis to get excited about, I am not sure what is.

Today's sell off is therefore, in my view, the chance that I did not believe would come around when I wrote my last "Hold" recommendation, i.e. to pick up Lilly stock at <$650 per share.

This could be a good time to "be greedy when others are fearful", although I'll acknowledge the strategy is a risky one, given current headwinds.
